name: Applied Digital Corporation

profile.description: Applied Digital Corporation designs, develops, and operates next-generation digital infrastructure for high-performance computing (HPC) and artificial intelligence workloads. The company builds and manages large-scale AI data center campuses ('AI Factories') with customers including CoreWeave and investment-grade hyperscalers. Applied Digital's flagship Polaris Forge 1 campus in Ellendale, North Dakota, hosts 400MW for CoreWeave, and its Polaris Forge 2 campus will deliver 200MW for a US hyperscaler, representing approximately $16B in contracted lease revenue over ~15 years.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Applied Digital pivoted from crypto mining infrastructure to AI data center development in 2023, a transformative business model shift that opened an entirely new revenue stream. Revenue grew from $8.5M (FY2022) to $319M TTM, with FQ3 2026 revenue of $126.6M beating analyst estimates of $76.6M by 65% [1]. The pivot to purpose-built AI factories was prescient and well-executed. AI Deployment (2/2): Applied Digital IS the AI infrastructure — its entire business is building and operating data centers for AI workloads. Polaris Forge 1 delivered 100MW on schedule for CoreWeave, and the company specializes in liquid cooling, high-voltage power distribution, and maximum GPU utilization for AI training [2]. Market Share (1/2): Applied Digital is a meaningful but smaller player in the AI data center market, competing against much larger operators like Equinix, Digital Realty, and QTS. The company's competitive advantage is speed-to-power in underserved rural markets with low energy costs, but it is not a category leader [2]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Polaris Forge 2 (200MW for an investment-grade hyperscaler, ~$5B revenue over 15 years) is under construction with phased delivery beginning 2026. The remaining 300MW at Polaris Forge 1 is expected complete by end 2027, totaling 600MW across campuses. Specific timelines and customer commitments provide strong visibility [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): Applied Digital invests heavily in innovative data center engineering including liquid cooling technology, high-density power distribution, and custom facility designs optimized for AI workloads, exceeding industry-standard approaches.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Applied Digital's purpose — building next-generation digital infrastructure to power AI and HPC workloads — is clear and identifies what the company does and for whom, but it doesn't articulate a deeper purpose beyond serving the AI buildout. The company describes itself as 'future-proof engineering' focused on 'solving hard problems,' which is earnest but not distinctive [2]. Community Impact (1/2): Applied Digital builds campuses in rural communities like Ellendale, North Dakota (population ~1,300), bringing significant economic development, jobs, and tax revenue to underserved areas. The rural data center strategy creates genuine community impact, though the primary motivation is low energy costs rather than community uplift [1]. Employee Retention (0/2): With only 205 employees, Applied Digital is a very small organization. No data available on retention rates, average tenure, or employee sentiment. The company hired Laura Laltrello as COO in 2025, bringing 20 years of data center expertise, suggesting leadership is building a capable team [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): Mixed insider activity — CEO Wes Cummins sold $6M in shares in January 2026 and additional shares in April 2026, while Directors Nottenburg and Miller also sold in April-May 2026. Large stock awards were granted to CFO Mohmand ($0) and President Zhang ($0). Net insider selling signals reduced conviction [4].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Applied Digital's Infrastructure reflects explosive revenue growth offset by deep unprofitability and negative cash flows typical of a pre-scale infrastructure buildout. D/E of 110% is elevated but below the 300% floor, with $2.8B in total debt including the recently issued $2.15B in 6.75% Senior Secured Notes due 2031 [1][4]. ROE is -5.6%, reflecting the company's unprofitable status as it invests heavily in campus construction. Revenue grew 139% YoY (FQ3 2026 revenue of $126.6M vs. prior year), and 3yr revenue CAGR exceeds 150% from $8.5M to $144M, scoring full marks on growth [1]. However, profit margin is -59.5% with GAAP net loss of $100.9M in FQ3 2026, Forward PE is negative (EPS forward -$0.70), and free cash flow is -$1.5B, reflecting massive construction capex. The $16B contracted revenue backlog provides future visibility but current financials are deeply loss-making [1]. Liquidity floor cleared: 21.5M avg volume x $41.25 = $888M daily.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Applied Digital IS AI infrastructure — its entire business model is building and operating data centers purpose-built for AI training and inference workloads. AI cannot disrupt what it fundamentally requires to exist. The company's $16B in contracted lease revenue from CoreWeave ($11B) and an investment-grade hyperscaler ($5B) represents 15-year commitments that are immune to AI substitution risk [1]. The physical infrastructure — power delivery systems, liquid cooling, fiber connectivity, and purpose-built buildings — cannot be replicated by software. Growing demand for AI compute creates a structural tailwind as model sizes and training compute requirements increase exponentially [2]. AI Opportunity (1/1): Applied Digital generates 100% of its revenue from AI and HPC infrastructure services. FQ3 2026 revenue of $126.6M was entirely AI-related, including $73M in tenant fit-out services and $12M in HPC rental revenues from CoreWeave's Polaris Forge 1 campus. The company is a pure-play AI infrastructure beneficiary [1].

infinite_game.summary: Applied Digital demonstrates genuine infinite game qualities through its transformative pivot from crypto mining to AI infrastructure and CEO Cummins' long-term ownership mindset. The $16B contracted backlog spanning 15 years reflects long-term thinking. However, the company is too young (founded 2022 in current form) to have been tested through crises, and heavy insider selling creates tension between stated long-term vision and near-term executive behavior.

just_cause.analysis: Applied Digital's cause — building the physical infrastructure that powers the AI revolution — is genuine, specific, and connects the company's work to a technological transformation that will shape the next several decades. CEO Cummins articulates a vision of 'future-proof engineering' and building infrastructure 'designed to perform over decades,' which reflects intentionality beyond quarterly results [2]. However, the cause is still young and untested through adversity. Score: 2 weighted points.

courage_to_lead.analysis: The pivot from crypto mining to AI data centers in 2023 required courage — walking away from an established revenue stream to bet on an emerging market before the AI buildout was proven. Raising $2.15B in debt to fund Polaris Forge 2 while still deeply unprofitable demonstrates willingness to sacrifice near-term profitability for long-term positioning. However, the company has not yet been tested through a downturn or industry slowdown [1]. Score: 1 weighted point.

trusting_teams.analysis: Applied Digital is a small team of 205 employees, and the hiring of experienced COO Laura Laltrello (20 years of data center expertise) suggests intentional team building. CEO Cummins brings 20+ years as a technology investor and has maintained a consistent leadership team through the company's rapid growth phase [2]. Limited data on organizational culture at this scale. Score: 1 weighted point.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Applied Digital's pivot from cryptocurrency mining infrastructure to AI data centers in 2023 is a textbook example of existential flexibility. The company recognized that its core competency — building and operating power-intensive computing facilities — could be redirected toward AI workloads, and executed the transformation before the AI infrastructure boom was consensus. Revenue shifted from crypto hosting to AI/HPC, growing from $55M to $319M TTM [1][2]. Score: 2 weighted points.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Applied Digital competes against much larger data center operators (Equinix, Digital Realty, QTS) and hyperscalers building their own facilities. CEO Cummins' competitive language focuses on speed-to-market and rural location advantages rather than beating rivals, but there is limited evidence of explicitly acknowledging competitors' strengths or using them as motivation to improve [2]. Score: 0 weighted points.

investment_thesis: Applied Digital is a pure-play AI infrastructure company with $16B in contracted lease revenue spanning ~15 years across two major campuses — $11B from CoreWeave at Polaris Forge 1 (400MW) and $5B from an investment-grade US hyperscaler at Polaris Forge 2 (200MW) [1]. FQ3 2026 revenue of $126.6M beat analyst estimates by 65%, growing 250% YoY as the company transitions from construction to recurring rental revenue. The 2023 pivot from crypto mining to AI data centers was prescient, and CEO Cummins' focus on speed-to-power in rural locations with low energy costs creates a cost advantage over competitors. The $2.15B senior secured notes offering funds Polaris Forge 2 construction with phased delivery beginning 2026 [1].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on execution risk, capital intensity, and customer concentration. Applied Digital is deeply unprofitable with a -59.5% profit margin, GAAP net loss of $100.9M in FQ3 2026, and -$1.5B in free cash flow as construction capex overwhelms revenue [4]. Total debt of $2.8B against D/E of 110% creates leverage risk if AI demand slows or construction timelines slip. Customer concentration is extreme — CoreWeave represents ~69% of contracted backlog ($11B of $16B), and CoreWeave itself carries a BB credit rating despite the SPV refinancing receiving an A3 rating [1]. CEO Cummins sold $6M in shares in January 2026 and additional shares in April, a concerning signal for a company asking investors to trust the long-term vision. The 205-employee headcount against $2.8B in debt and multi-year construction commitments creates key-person and organizational capacity risk [2][4].
key_catalysts: Transition from construction-phase revenue to recurring rental income as Polaris Forge 1 buildings energize sequentially through 2027, providing path to profitability with $11B in CoreWeave contracted revenue [1]
Polaris Forge 2 construction funded by $2.15B senior notes, with 200MW delivery to investment-grade hyperscaler beginning 2026, adding $5B in contracted revenue visibility [1]
Secular AI infrastructure buildout driving exponential demand for purpose-built GPU compute facilities, with Applied Digital positioned as a speed-to-power specialist in underserved rural markets [2]
key_risks: Extreme customer concentration — CoreWeave represents $11B of $16B contracted backlog (69%), and CoreWeave's BB credit rating creates counterparty risk despite the A3-rated SPV structure [1]
Deep unprofitability with -59.5% profit margin, -$1.5B free cash flow, and $2.8B total debt creating existential risk if AI demand slows or construction timelines slip [4]
Heavy insider selling by CEO Cummins ($6M+ in 2026) and multiple directors contradicts the long-term ownership narrative and signals potential near-term dilution or execution concerns [4]
sources: Applied Digital FQ3 2026 Earnings Release — revenue $126.6M (beat $76.6M estimate), GAAP net loss $100.9M (-$0.36/share), $16B contracted backlog, CoreWeave lease restructuring, $2.15B senior notes offering; Applied Digital IR, GlobeNewswire, Investing.com, April 2026

Applied Digital CEO Wes Cummins leadership profile — AI data center strategy, NVIDIA partnership, Laura Laltrello COO hire, 600MW campus expansion, speed-to-power competitive advantage; D Magazine, RCR Wireless, Applied Digital website, 2025-2026
Applied Digital ESG — no specific controversies identified; general AI data center energy consumption concerns apply to the sector broadly; 2025-2026
